proofs.proof_tex:
Put \(q_j=\pi_{1j}\) for \(1\le j\le5\) and \(v_k=\pi_{2k}\) for \(1\le k\le3\). Under \(\Gamma=2\), these vectors lie in their respective simplices and satisfy \(q_j\le2q_\ell\) and \(v_k\le2v_\ell\) for every pair of coordinates. The product-law assumption in \(\mathcal P_2\) makes the probability of cell \((j,k)\) equal to \(q_jv_k\). The score sums are
\[ egin{array}{c|ccc} &6&5&-2\ \hline -4&2&1&-6\ -4&2&1&-6\ -1&5&4&-3\ -1&5&4&-3\ 1&7&6&-1 \end{array}\]
where the row and column labels are the first- and second-set scores. Consequently,
\[A_+(6;d^\dagger)=\{(5,1),(5,2)\},\qquad A_-(6;d^\dagger)=\{(1,3),(2,3)\}.\]
For the upper tail, every admissible law therefore has probability \(q_5(v_1+v_2)\). Since \(q_j\ge q_5/2\) for \(j
e5\),
\[1=\sum_{j=1}^5q_j\ge q_5+4 rac{q_5}{2}=3q_5,\qquad q_5\le rac13.\]
Writing \(u=v_1+v_2\), the inequalities \(v_1,v_2\le2v_3\) give \(u\le4v_3=4(1-u)\), and hence \(u\le4/5\). Thus \(P\{A_+(6;d^\dagger)\}\le4/15\). Equality is attained by
\[q^+=\left( rac16, rac16, rac16, rac16, rac13ight),\qquad v^+=\left( rac25, rac25, rac15ight),\]
whose coordinate ratios are at most two. It follows that \(p_+(6;d^\dagger,2)=4/15\).

For the lower tail, put \(y=q_1+q_2\) and \(M=\max(q_1,q_2)\). The odds inequalities imply \(q_3+q_4+q_5\ge3M/2\), while \(M\ge y/2\). Therefore
\[1=y+(q_3+q_4+q_5)\ge y+ rac{3y}{4}= rac{7y}{4},\qquad y\le rac47.\]
Also \(v_1,v_2\ge v_3/2\), so \(1=v_1+v_2+v_3\ge2v_3\) and \(v_3\le1/2\). Hence \(P\{A_-(6;d^\dagger)\}=yv_3\le2/7\). Equality is attained by
\[q^-=\left( rac27, rac27, rac17, rac17, rac17ight),\qquad v^-=\left( rac14, rac14, rac12ight),\]
so \(p_-(6;d^\dagger,2)=2/7\).

It remains to optimize the two tails under one shared law. Set \(x=q_5\) and again let \(y=q_1+q_2\) and \(u=v_1+v_2\). The displayed cell characterization gives
\[P\{A_J(6;d^\dagger)\}=xu+yv_3=y+(x-y)u.\]
Because \(q_1,q_2\ge x/2\), one has \(y\ge x\). Because \(v_1,v_2\ge v_3/2\), one has \(u\ge v_3=1-u\), hence \(u\ge1/2\). Since \(x-y\le0\),
\[y+(x-y)u\le y+ rac{x-y}{2}= rac{x+y}{2}.\]
Now let \(M_J=\max(q_1,q_2,q_5)\). The odds inequalities give \(q_3+q_4\ge M_J\), and \(M_J\ge(x+y)/3\). Consequently,
\[1=(x+y)+(q_3+q_4)\ge rac43(x+y),\qquad x+y\le rac34.\]
Thus every shared law has joint-tail probability at most \(3/8\). The admissible vectors
\[q^J=\left( rac14, rac14, rac18, rac18, rac14ight),\qquad v^J=\left( rac14, rac14, rac12ight)\]
attain equality: here \(x=1/4\), \(y=1/2\), and \(u=1/2\). Hence \(p_J(6;d^\dagger,2)=3/8\). Finally, \(4/15<2/7\) and \(8/15<1\), so the definition of the doubled-tail calibration yields
\[p_B(6;d^\dagger,2)=2\min\left\{ rac4{15}, rac27ight\}= rac8{15}.\]
Therefore
\[G(6;d^\dagger,2)= rac8{15}- rac38= rac{19}{120}=0.158\overline{3}\approx0.158,\]
as claimed.
